messages.content: Always use code blocks with the appropriate language tags. If asked for a table always format it using Markdown syntax.
fork  a repo using github cli

To fork a repository using the GitHub CLI, you can use the following command:

```bash
gh repo fork owner/repo
```

Replace `owner` with the GitHub username of the repository owner and `repo` with the name of the repository you want to fork.

Make sure you have the GitHub CLI installed and configured with your GitHub account before running this command.
list branches using git in cli

To list branches in a Git repository using the command line interface, you can use the following command:

```bash
git branch
```

This command will list all the branches in the repository. The current branch will be indicated with an asterisk (*).

If you want to see remote branches as well, you can use the following command:

```bash
git branch -a
```

This command will list both local and remote branches.

If you want a more compact view, you can use the `--list` option as well:

```bash
git branch --list
```

Git provides several subcommands related to branches that you can use in the command line interface. Here are some commonly used branch subcommands:

1. **Create a new branch:** To create a new branch and switch to it, you can use the following command:
   ```bash
   git checkout -b new-branch-name
   ```

2. **Switch to an existing branch:** To switch to an existing branch, you can use the `checkout` command followed by the branch name:
   ```bash
   git checkout branch-name
   ```

3. **Delete a branch:** To delete a branch, you can use the following command. Be cautious as this operation is irreversible.
   ```bash
   git branch -d branch-name
   ```

4. **Merge branches:** To merge another branch into your current branch, you can use the following command:
   ```bash
   git merge branch-name
   ```

5. **Rename a branch:** To rename the current branch, you can use the following commands:
   ```bash
   git branch -m new-branch-name
   ```

6. **List remote branches:** To list remote branches, you can use the following command:
   ```bash
   git branch -r
   ```

7. **Track a remote branch:** To create and start tracking a remote branch in Git, you can use the following commands:
   ```bash
   git checkout --track origin/branch-name
   ```

These are some of the common branch subcommands in Git that you can use in the command line interface.
You're welcome! If you have any more questions or need further assistance in the future, feel free to ask. Happy coding!
